author: Maham Fatima
title: TD Cowen Lowers Cerence (CRNC) PT to $12 Following Noisy FQ1 2026 Results
description: TD Cowen has reduced its price target for Cerence (CRNC) to $12 from $13, maintaining a Hold rating after characterizing the company's Q1 2026 results as "noisy." Despite a significant year-over-year revenue surge driven by a patent settlement and reaffirmed full-year guidance, the firm noted a potential EBITDA shortfall for the rest of the year compared to consensus. Cerence reported a 126% revenue increase in Q1 2026, largely due to a $49.5 million patent settlement with Samsung, and is advancing its next-generation XUI platform and expanding its AI portfolio.

author: Motley Fool
title: Will Taking Over Apple's Credit Card Business Boost JPMorgan Chase Stock?
description: JPMorgan Chase is set to take over the Apple Card business from Goldman Sachs in early 2026, a transition expected to take about two years. While the Apple Card user base of over 12 million is not a massive expansion for Chase, which already issues 150 million cards, a significant portion of these users are young. This demographic presents valuable cross-selling opportunities for Chase's other products and is anticipated to positively impact the bank's stock.

author: Scott Kanowsky
title: A "breakdown" in U.S. growth and hiring could impact Fed policy - Deutsche Bank
description: Analysts at Deutsche Bank Research note a "breakdown" in the historical correlation between U.S. economic growth and hiring trends since the COVID-19 pandemic. This divergence has led to the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates despite solid growth, influenced by a weak labor market and negative household sentiment. The resolution of this disparity will be crucial for future Fed policy decisions and could even affect the outcome of midterm elections, depending on whether hiring catches up to growth, or growth slows to meet hiring.

author: Khadija Saeed
title: Alibaba stock ends skid after Friday jump as Qwen giveaway hits glitches and earnings near
description: Alibaba's U.S.-listed shares climbed roughly 3% on Friday, recovering after a volatile period, partly due to investor re-entry into tech stocks. The company's AI ambitions gained attention as its Qwen app experienced a surge in users following a 3 billion yuan Lunar New Year giveaway, briefly knocking the app offline and facing restrictions on WeChat for sharing campaign links. Despite these glitches and privacy concerns, Qwen rapidly rose in the App Store, and Alibaba is now looking ahead to its quarterly earnings release on February 19th, with investors particularly interested in the sustained momentum of the Qwen rollout and spending patterns.

author: NLPC Staff
title: NLPC Urges Apple Investors to Vote for Accountability Regarding China Risk
description: The National Legal and Policy Center (NLPC) is advocating for Apple investors to support Proposal No. 5, which calls for a "China Entanglement Audit" from Apple's board of directors. Despite withdrawing its own climate-related shareholder proposal, NLPC emphasizes the need for transparency regarding the risks associated with Apple's significant manufacturing presence in China ahead of the annual meeting on February 24. They argue that shareholders need this report to accurately assess investment risks, given Apple's deep ties to the Chinese Communist Party's political and economic decisions.

title: Apple and Google Agree to App Store Changes After U.K. Scrutiny
description: Apple and Alphabet have agreed to implement changes to their app stores to enhance transparency and competition, following scrutiny from the U.K.'s Competition and Markets Authority (CMA). The CMA stated that these commitments will ensure fairer app reviews and provide developers with greater access to tools for creating competing products. This decision addresses concerns over the dominant control exerted by Apple's App Store and Google's Play Store within the mobile ecosystem.

title: Apple and Google pledge to improve app fairness

Both companies have committed to not discriminating against other developers' apps, ranking applications transparently, and providing developers with greater access to iOS features to foster competition within the U.K.'s app economy.

title: The most unexpected winner of the AI boom? Caterpillar
description: Caterpillar, traditionally known for construction vehicles, has emerged as a surprising beneficiary of the AI boom, with its stock doubling in the past year to an all-time high. The company's growing exposure to data centers, energy infrastructure, and AI-related demand, supplying critical equipment rather than developing AI itself, has driven its market capitalization to $347 billion. Its record full-year sales of $67.6 billion and a $51 billion order backlog underscore strong demand and a shift towards energy and power solutions, outperforming tech giants like Apple and Microsoft in stock growth.

title: Apple’s Japan App Store Shift Allows Third-Party Payments Amid Criticism
description: Apple has adjusted its App Store policies in Japan, allowing third-party payments to comply with new competition laws. However, critics and developers argue these changes are largely superficial due to ongoing commissions and technical hurdles, making the alternative economically unviable for many. This move comes amid increasing global regulatory scrutiny of Apple's App Store practices, with many claiming it merely repackages existing monopolistic controls rather than dismantling them.

title: Bad Bunny’s Apple Music Super Bowl LX Halftime Show sees 39% household viewership drop vs. last year, Samba TV data shows
description: Samba TV data indicates that Bad Bunny's Apple Music Super Bowl LX Halftime Show drew 26.5 million U.S. households, a 39% decrease compared to Kendrick Lamar's 2025 performance. Despite this, Nielsen reported 128.2 million viewers for Bad Bunny’s show, a figure only slightly lower than the previous year and representing the second-most-watched Super Bowl telecast in U.S. history. The show, largely in Spanish, was the most-watched Super Bowl halftime in Spanish-language history.

author: Daragh Thomas
title: Odds Trump Is Forced To Refund Tariffs Drop Sharply As Supreme Court Justice Warns Of Legal Complexity
description: Odds of former President Trump being forced to refund recently imposed tariffs have fallen sharply to 28% after Supreme Court Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson warned of the legal complexity and extended timeline for any potential ruling. Her comments suggest a decision is unlikely before the Polymarket contract expires on June 30, affecting companies like Best Buy (NYSE: BBY), General Motors (NYSE: GM), Apple (NASDAQ: AAPL), Tesla (NASDAQ: TSLA), Target (NYSE: TGT), Toyota (NYSE: TM), and Honda (NYSE: HMC) which are highly sensitive to tariff developments. The "no refund" outcome is paradoxically bullish for bonds like the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ: TLT) as it secures revenue for the federal budget.

title: \'Double-dipping\' dispute over Qualcomm iPhone chips finally ends
description: A class-action lawsuit in the UK against Qualcomm, alleging "double-dipping" practices that inflated iPhone prices, has been abandoned. This dispute claimed Qualcomm charged Apple twice for radio chips—once for the chip and again for a patent license based on a percentage of the iPhone's price. Although Apple settled its own claims with Qualcomm in 2024, this independent lawsuit, brought on behalf of 29 million iPhone and Samsung owners seeking £480 million, had continued until now.

Although Apple settled its own claims with Qualcomm in 2024, this independent lawsuit, brought on behalf of 29 million iPhone and Samsung owners seeking £480 million, had continued until now.

author: Sam Boughedda
title: This is the most under-owned megacap tech stock, according to Morgan Stanley
description: Morgan Stanley analysts have identified Nvidia as the most "under-owned" large-cap tech stock, despite its significant role in AI. Their analysis of Q4 13F filings revealed that mega-cap tech stocks are broadly under-owned, with institutional investors showing a preference for AI "picks and shovels" hardware and semiconductor companies while having notably low positions in software names. SanDisk (SNDK) was highlighted as having the largest over-ownership gap among large-cap tech stocks after its re-listing and S&P 500 inclusion.

author: Douglas A. McIntyre
title: Buffett Dumps More Apple
description: Warren Buffett, in his last quarter as Berkshire Hathaway CEO, continued to trim his position in Apple, a trend he has maintained for several quarters. While Apple's stock has seen an 8% increase over the past year, it lags behind the S&P and Alphabet, and its iPhone sales remain 60% of its revenue. One likely reason for Buffett's divestment could be Apple's apparent lag in the AI race, relying on licensing Google Gemini for Siri instead of developing its own substantial AI initiatives.

author: Austin Smith
title: The 2x Leveraged SSO ETF Can Double Your Returns, Until Volatility Strikes
description: The ProShares Ultra S&P500 (SSO) is a 2x leveraged ETF designed to amplify S&P 500 returns daily, but it's built for short-term tactical trading rather than long-term holding due to volatility drag from daily rebalancing. While SSO can deliver amplified gains in sustained uptrends (like 1.8x over five years in a bull market), choppy markets erode returns, and volatility can lead to significant intraday swings and magnified losses. Its portfolio is technology-heavy, with NVIDIA and Apple as top holdings, making it a magnified bet on mega-cap tech leadership.

title: Mark Zuckerberg said he reached out to Apple CEO Tim Cook to discuss 'wellbeing of teens and kids'
description: Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg testified in a social media safety trial, revealing he contacted Apple CEO Tim Cook in 2018 to discuss the well-being of teenagers and children. Zuckerberg defended Meta's stance on free expression and emphasized their efforts to remove underage users, despite acknowledging challenges in age verification and some internal disagreements on product decisions.

author: Jonny Evans
title: Apple to kill app support for Intel-based Macs next year
description: Apple has announced that macOS 26 will be the last operating system version to support Intel-based Macs, and Rosetta support for Intel-based apps will end with macOS 28 in 2027. This means companies and users relying on Intel apps or Macs need to prepare for upgrades or replacements to ensure business continuity and security. While security updates will continue for some older Mac models for two years post-support, and Rosetta will remain for certain unmaintained games, most developers have already transitioned to Apple Silicon, and Apple sees this move as a benefit for performance and productivity.

author: Anuja Vaidya
title: Apple Watch could miss hypertension diagnoses in seniors
description: New research indicates that the Apple Watch's hypertension notification feature may miss diagnoses in older adults, who are at a higher risk for the condition, and also shows variations in alert accuracy across different racial and ethnic groups. While the feature can increase the probability of identifying hypertension, a significant proportion of undiagnosed individuals may still not receive an alert. Researchers emphasize that the smartwatch feature should supplement, not replace, standard blood pressure screening methods.

author: Eileen McDermott
title: British Consumer Association Withdraws Competition Claim Against Qualcomm
description: A British consumers' organization, Which?, has withdrawn its competition claim against Qualcomm after concluding that the Competition Appeal Tribunal would likely find Qualcomm not at fault. The claim, filed in February 2021 on behalf of around 29 million British consumers, alleged that Qualcomm had breached UK competition law by charging inflated fees for technology licenses, which were then passed on to consumers as higher smartphone prices. Qualcomm welcomed the withdrawal, stating that it reaffirms judicial holdings that its licensing practices are lawful and do not harm competition.

author: Lora Kolodny
title: Apple sued by West Virginia for alleged failure to stop child sexual abuse material on iCloud, iOS devices
description: West Virginia has filed a consumer protection lawsuit against Apple, alleging the company has failed to prevent child sexual abuse materials (CSAM) on its iOS devices and iCloud services. Attorney General John "JB" McCuskey criticized Apple for prioritizing privacy branding over child safety, in contrast to competitors, and is seeking damages and injunctive relief to force Apple to implement effective CSAM detection. Apple stated that protecting users' safety and privacy, especially children, is central to its mission, highlighting existing features like Communications Safety and parental controls.

Attorney General John "JB" McCuskey criticized Apple for prioritizing privacy branding over child safety, in contrast to competitors, and is seeking damages and injunctive relief to force Apple to implement effective CSAM detection. Apple stated that protecting users' safety and privacy, especially children, is central to its mission, highlighting existing features like Communications Safety and parental controls.

author: JEFF HADEN
title: Steve Jobs Saved Apple by Overcoming ‘Divestiture Aversion.’ It Will Work for You, Too
description: This article discusses "divestiture aversion," a common human tendency to overvalue items we own, making it difficult to let them go. It highlights how Steve Jobs exemplified overcoming this by drastically cutting Apple's product line upon his return in 1997, saving the company from bankruptcy. The piece suggests that understanding and counteracting divestiture aversion can be beneficial for individuals and businesses.

author: Ramishah Maruf, Clare Duffy
title: State sues Apple for negligence over the alleged distribution of child sexual abuse materials on iCloud and devices
description: The West Virginia attorney general's office has sued Apple, alleging the tech giant enabled the storage and distribution of child sexual abuse materials (CSAM) on iCloud by prioritizing user privacy over child safety. The lawsuit claims Apple's reporting of CSAM is significantly lower than other tech companies and demands statutory and punitive damages as well as injunctive relief for effective detection measures. Apple, countering, emphasized its commitment to safety and privacy, highlighting features like Communication Safety, while critics argue its past decision to abandon a more robust detection tool like NeuralHash shows negligence.
content: The West Virginia attorney general's office has sued Apple, alleging the tech giant enabled the storage and distribution of child sexual abuse materials (CSAM) on iCloud by prioritizing user privacy over child safety. The lawsuit claims Apple's reporting of CSAM is significantly lower than other tech companies and demands statutory and punitive damages as well as injunctive relief for effective detection measures. Apple, countering, emphasized its commitment to safety and privacy, highlighting features like Communication Safety, while critics argue its past decision to abandon a more robust detection tool like NeuralHash shows negligence.

author: The National Center
title: Peeling Back the Apple: Shareholder Activists Seek to Expose Company's China Entanglements
description: Ahead of Apple, Inc.'s annual shareholder meeting, the National Center for Public Policy Research’s Free Enterprise Project (FEP) is urging investors to support a "China Entanglement Audit." The proposed audit seeks transparency on financial exposure under high tariffs, risks from regulatory actions, intellectual property theft, and the costs of diversifying supply chains away from China.

description: West Virginia Attorney General JB McCuskey has filed a lawsuit against Apple Inc., alleging the company knowingly allowed its iCloud platform to be used for distributing and storing child sexual abuse material (CSAM). McCuskey is seeking statutory and punitive damages, injunctive relief, and equitable remedies for safer product design.

author: Shyla Florence
title: Apple knowingly ignored child sexual abuse material on iCloud, West Virginia lawsuit alleges
description: West Virginia Attorney General JB McCuskey has filed a lawsuit against Apple Inc., alleging the company knowingly allowed its iCloud platform to be used for child sexual abuse material distribution without reporting it to federal authorities. The lawsuit contends that Apple prioritized user privacy over child safety by failing to implement standard detection tools, despite internal communications describing iCloud as a significant platform for such material. The state is seeking damages and injunctive relief to compel Apple to adopt effective detection measures and safer product designs.

The state is seeking damages and injunctive relief to compel Apple to adopt effective detection measures and safer product designs.

author: Michael Kan
title: West Virginia Sues Apple Over Alleged Child Sexual Abuse Content on iCloud
description: West Virginia is suing Apple, alleging that the company "knowingly allowed its iCloud platform to be used as a vehicle for distributing and storing child sexual abuse material" (CSAM). The lawsuit seeks to compel Apple to implement a system for tracking CSAM on iCloud, years after the company abandoned a similar system due to privacy concerns. The state argues that Apple's end-to-end encryption for iCloud provides a sanctuary for such illegal content, making it inaccessible to law enforcement.

author: Natalie Lung
title: Google Gemini, Apple add music-focused generative AI features
description: Google and Apple are enhancing their core consumer apps with music-focused generative AI features. Google's Gemini AI assistant can now create 30-second music tracks and custom cover art using Google DeepMind's Lyria 3 model, available to users 18 and older, with usage limits based on subscription tier. Apple Music is introducing "Playlist Playground" which allows users to create playlists from text prompts, integrated with Apple Intelligence and rolling out in iOS 26.4.

author: Stan Schroeder
title: Sonos' big comeback is a streaming box
description: After a challenging year for Sonos, marked by a problematic app revamp, the company is reportedly planning a major new product: an Android-based streaming box codenamed Pinewood. This device, expected to launch in 2025 and priced between $200 and $400, will offer a unified streaming experience from various platforms and integrate seamlessly with Sonos' existing audio ecosystem. Despite strong competition in the streaming box market, this product is seen as Sonos' crucial step to regain user trust and enter a new growth category.

author: David Edwards
title: Kodiak AI adds Haas Alert’s ‘Safety Cloud’ to its autonomous trucking solution
description: Kodiak AI has integrated Haas Alert's Safety Cloud digital alerting platform into its autonomous trucking solution to enhance roadway safety. This collaboration allows Kodiak's autonomous trucks to send real-time safety alerts to approaching vehicles through cellular networks, appearing on infotainment screens or digital devices. The integration aims to provide an additional layer of safety beyond current regulatory requirements and supports the wider adoption of autonomous trucking technology.

author: Greg Jordan
title: W.Va. sues Apple over alleged child abuse material on iCloud
description: West Virginia's Attorney General, J.B. McCuskey, announced a lawsuit against Apple Inc. for allegedly allowing its iCloud platform to be used for distributing and storing child sexual abuse material (CSAM). The lawsuit claims Apple knowingly allowed this activity and failed to report detected CSAM, unlike other tech companies that filed millions of reports. McCuskey stated that Apple prioritized user privacy over protecting children and is seeking statutory and punitive damages, injunctive relief, and equitable remedies to mandate safer product design.

author: Ramishah Maruf, Clare Duffy, CNN
title: West Virginia sues Apple over alleged distribution of child sexual abuse materials on iCloud and devices
description: The West Virginia Attorney General's office has sued Apple, alleging that the tech giant has allowed child sexual abuse materials (CSAM) to be stored and distributed on its iCloud service for years, prioritizing user privacy over child safety. The lawsuit claims Apple's reporting of CSAM is significantly lower than other tech companies and accuses the company of negligence for not implementing adequate detection technologies like PhotoDNA. Apple maintains its commitment to user safety and privacy, highlighting its Communication Safety feature, while the lawsuit seeks damages and mandating effective detection measures.

title: David Rolfe Trims Apple: Wedgewood’s 2026 Tech Valuation Strategy

author: Jonathan Vanian
title: Meta and Apple face serious questions about child safety and privacy
description: Meta and Apple are facing intense scrutiny regarding child safety and privacy on their platforms, stemming from legal proceedings in California, New Mexico, and West Virginia. Unsealed documents from Meta's New Mexico case reveal internal discussions about 7.5 million child sexual abuse material reports that would no longer be disclosed due to the company's shift to end-to-end encryption on Messenger. Meanwhile, Apple faces a lawsuit in West Virginia alleging insufficient efforts to prevent child sexual abuse material on its iOS devices and iCloud services.

Meanwhile, Apple faces a lawsuit in West Virginia alleging insufficient efforts to prevent child sexual abuse material on its iOS devices and iCloud services.

title: BOE Poised to Become Apple’s Top MacBook Display Panel Supplier in 2025, Says Omdia
description: Omdia predicts that BOE will become Apple's leading MacBook display panel supplier in 2025, securing 51% of the market share. While LG Display's share is expected to decrease, Sharp will continue supplying MacBook Pro panels, and Samsung Display is anticipated to join the MacBook supply chain in 2026 with the introduction of OLED technology.

title: Apple paying $95 million after class-action lawsuit over “unintended” use of Siri across devices

Eligible consumers who filed claims before July 2, 2025, may receive up to $20 per Siri device, with payments already underway.

title: SPY, IVV, VOO Battle for Top ETF Crown Amid S&P 500 Swings
description: The S&P 500 experienced significant volatility and a market correction following a recent tariff announcement, leading to a re-evaluation of its valuation. This market movement has intensified the competition among the three major S&P 500 tracking ETFs—SPY, IVV, and VOO—for asset under management (AUM) supremacy. While all three track the same index, differences in expense ratios, asset gathering, structure (UIT vs. open-end fund), and tax efficiency distinguish them for investors.

author: Ramishah Maruf, Clare Duffy
title: West Virginia sues Apple over alleged distribution of child sexual abuse materials on iCloud and devices
description: The West Virginia attorney general's office has sued Apple, claiming the company allowed child sexual abuse materials (CSAM) to be stored and distributed on its iCloud service, prioritizing user privacy over child safety. The lawsuit highlights Apple's significantly lower reporting rates of CSAM compared to other tech giants like Google and criticizes Apple for abandoning its NeuralHash detection tool due to privacy concerns. West Virginia is seeking damages and demanding that Apple implement more effective CSAM detection measures.

author: Andy Chalk
title: Take-Two Interactive says in-game currencies are 'fictions' that players can't actually own
description: Take-Two Interactive is seeking to dismiss a lawsuit alleging "civil theft" and unfair business practices regarding in-game currencies in its NBA 2K games. The company argues that virtual currencies are "fictions" subject to game terms and conditions, not property owned by players. This defense suggests that players suffer no economic injury when these currencies disappear as older game versions are retired, leading to a discussion about the contrast with more independent cryptocurrencies.

author: Jack Shannon
title: Mutual Fund Managers Are Wrong More Than They’re Right
description: This article analyzes mutual fund managers' "hit rates" (percentage of holdings outperforming their benchmark index) and finds that most managers pick more losing stocks than winners. While higher hit rates generally correlate with less underperformance, the study concludes that portfolio construction, specifically the weighting of winning stocks, is more crucial than merely picking winners. Market returns are concentrated, meaning a few big winners drive most gains, and past hit rates are not reliable predictors of future success for mutual fund managers or individual investors.

Users are advised to check with their carrier to see if their devices are affected and may require software updates or upgrades to maintain service.

author: Malathi Nayak, Mark Gurman and Jef Feeley / Bloomberg
title: Apple Faces Revenue Challenge as Judge Orders App Store Changes
description: A federal judge has ordered Apple to allow developers to direct consumers to outside payment methods, challenging the company's 30% commission on App Store transactions. While the ruling found Apple engaged in anticompetitive conduct by preventing developers from offering cheaper prices, it stopped short of declaring the App Store a monopoly or allowing third-party app marketplaces. This decision, though not going as far as Epic Games sought, is a significant setback for Apple and adds to global pressure from regulators regarding its market dominance.

author: La rédaction
title: West Virginia Files Lawsuit Against Apple Over Alleged CSAM Oversight
description: West Virginia has sued Apple Inc., alleging the company failed to adequately prevent child sexual abuse material (CSAM) from being stored and shared on its iCloud platform and devices. The lawsuit contends that Apple's privacy-focused approach hindered its ability to detect and report illegal content, contrasting its reporting rates with those of other tech companies. State officials are seeking financial penalties and court orders for Apple to enhance its CSAM detection, monitoring, and reporting procedures.

author: Austin Smith
title: AAPD’s 3.43% Yield Looks Great Until You See the 40% Hangover

Investors should be wary of treating AAPD as an income vehicle given its declining distributions and nearly 40% price loss since August 2022.

title: Apple quietly removes environmental metrics from executive pay
description: Apple has quietly removed environmental metrics from its executive pay packages for 2025, a trend mirroring similar decisions by other major companies like Starbucks and Salesforce. This move is part of a broader shift away from tying executive compensation to environmental, social, and governance (ESG) goals, influenced by political opposition and a decrease in investor pressure. While companies insist on continued commitment to environmental goals, experts express concern that removing these links could reduce leadership attention to crucial sustainability efforts.

author: Arundhati Sarkar
title: Apple more confident in Mac Mini Houston plans than Mac Pro Austin; moves to expand U.S. chip production
description: Apple is prioritizing its Mac Mini manufacturing expansion in Houston over its Mac Pro operations in Austin, aiming to strengthen domestic production. The company is collaborating with Foxconn to establish a 200,000 square-foot Mac Mini assembly facility in Houston and is also investing in U.S. chip production by increasing purchases from TSMC's Arizona plant and supporting Amkor Technology's new chip packaging facilities. These efforts highlight Apple's strategic push towards reshoring its supply chain and enhancing confidence in its U.S. manufacturing capabilities.

title: Pasadena Man’s Wrongful Termination Suit Against Apple Returns to Court
description: A lawsuit filed by Pasadena resident Robert Bailey against Apple Inc. for alleged wrongful termination, discrimination, and retaliation is returning to a Los Angeles courtroom. Bailey, who worked for Apple for nearly a decade, claims he was fired in November 2023 after taking medical leave and for supposedly violating a policy he was never trained on and was denied access to. He is seeking at least $500,000 in compensatory damages, plus punitive damages, alleging his termination was due to his health conditions, accommodation requests, and use of medical leave.

title: Form 424B2 BARCLAYS BANK PLC
description: Barclays Bank PLC has filed Form 424B2 for Autocallable Leveraged Barrier Notes due February 25, 2031, linked to the common stock of Apple Inc., Broadcom Inc., and Alphabet Inc. These notes do not pay interest and do not guarantee the full principal at maturity, exposing investors to potential significant losses if not automatically redeemed and if the least performing underlier falls below its barrier value. The document details key terms, risk considerations, hypothetical payment examples, and tax implications, emphasizing the notes' speculative nature and the issuer's credit risk.

Shareholders adhered to Apple's guidance, also approving the election of the board, ratification of Ernst & Young, executive compensation, and the non-employee director stock plan.

title: Spain's antitrust watchdog says Apple, Amazon took too long to refine anti-competitive contracts
description: Spain's competition regulator, CNMC, has ruled that Apple and Amazon took too long to remove anti-competitive clauses from their contracts, a delay which could lead to new fines. The companies were previously fined 194 million euros in July 2023 for these clauses, which restricted Apple resellers and advertising of competing products on Amazon's Spanish website. Although the original fine is suspended pending appeal, the CNMC is considering a fresh probe due to the companies' failure to remove the clauses until May 2025.

author: Andrew Orr
title: Advanced Apple Silicon remains tied to Taiwan despite Arizona fab expansion
description: Apple's expansion of semiconductor manufacturing in Arizona and Texas is primarily aimed at reducing geopolitical risk and strengthening supply chain resilience rather than bringing back large-scale U.S. factory jobs. While TSMC's Arizona fab can produce some Apple Silicon chips like the A16, advanced chips such as the A19 in the iPhone 17 and M5 still rely on Taiwan due to its leading process nodes, engineering talent, and established supply networks. The U.S. strategy is a long-term hedge to protect against supply disruptions, but Taiwan remains central to Apple's most sophisticated silicon manufacturing.

title: Apple to buy more than 100 million chips from TSMC's Arizona fab by year's end
description: Apple Inc. announced plans to purchase over 100 million computer chips from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.'s (TSMC) Arizona fab site by the end of the year. This chip acquisition is part of Apple's larger $600 billion U.S. manufacturing commitment with TSMC, which is expanding its facility in north Phoenix. Apple has also invested in Amkor's new semiconductor packaging factory being built in Peoria.

title: Apple to Roll Out Made in America Mac Mini as Part of Trump Spend Push
description: Apple announced it will begin manufacturing its Mac mini desktop computers in Houston later this year, expanding its U.S. production footprint. This move is part of a larger $600 billion U.S. investment commitment made during a meeting between CEO Tim Cook and former President Donald Trump. While the Mac mini is a lower-volume product, its production in the U.S. is seen as an effort to bolster American manufacturing and potentially mitigate tariff issues, following previous efforts with the Mac Pro.

title: Profit slump and rising risks at STMicroelectronics (NYSE: STM)
description: STMicroelectronics (NYSE: STM) reported a significant financial downturn for 2025, with net revenues falling to $11.8 billion and net income plummeting to $180 million due to weaker demand and substantial impairment and restructuring costs. Despite maintaining a strong balance sheet and dividend, the company faces rising risks including semiconductor cyclicality, trade pressures, supply chain constraints, and customer concentration, particularly with Apple. STMicroelectronics is implementing a company-wide program to reshape its manufacturing footprint and resize its cost base to improve operating efficiency and strengthen capability.

author: David Seeley
title: MP Materials To Build '10X,' a $1.25B Rare Earth Magnet Manufacturing Campus in North Texas
description: MP Materials announced it will invest over $1.25 billion to construct a 120-acre rare earth magnet manufacturing campus, named "10X," in Northlake, North Texas. This project, which aims to be the largest such facility in U.S. history, will create over 1,500 jobs and significantly bolster America's rare earth magnet supply chain independence, with commissioning slated for 2028. The expansion is supported by approximately $200 million in incentives from Texas, Denton County, and Northlake, and builds upon MP Materials' existing facility in Fort Worth and its partnership with the Department of Defense.

author: Dan Lefkovitz
title: US Stock Market Concentration Has Surpassed Its 1930s Peak. Should Investors Worry?
description: The US stock market is currently experiencing a level of concentration in its top 10 stocks that has surpassed its 1932 peak, drawing parallels to the "Roaring '20s" due to technological transformations, high wealth inequality, and increased credit. While this concentration doesn't automatically signal a bubble and has historically led to strong returns, it does heighten risk by eroding diversification and making markets more sensitive to sentiment shifts. The author suggests investors consider diversification strategies, such as selective stock-picking, equal-weighting, or balancing US exposure with international equities and other asset classes, to mitigate these risks.

It also provides a significant credibility boost for Apple in government and defense sectors, though it does not extend to higher NATO classification levels.

Any interruption to TSMC's advanced chip exports could widely impact global technology hardware and services. While TSMC's scale and technological edge give it a dominant position, its concentration in Taiwan creates a single point of failure that governments worldwide are trying to mitigate through massive investments in new fabrication plants outside of Taiwan.

description: A judge dismissed a lawsuit against Apple Inc. filed by Robert Bailey, who alleged disability discrimination after being fired in 2023 at age 79. The judge ruled that Apple provided a legitimate business reason for the termination and found no evidence that the policy violation was an excuse to fire Bailey due to his health.

author: Reuters
title: Nasdaq seeks SEC approval for prediction markets options on major stock index
description: Nasdaq has filed for SEC approval to introduce prediction market options on its Nasdaq 100 index and Nasdaq 100 micro index. These binary options, called "Outcome-Related Options," allow traders to bet on the yes-or-no outcomes of specific events, reflecting a growing interest among exchange operators in the event prediction markets space. While CME Group has already expanded into this area, SEC-monitored entities like Nasdaq and Cboe have proceeded more cautiously due to the evolving regulatory landscape.

title: The Memory Supercycle: Why Micron Technology (MU) is the Indispensable Engine of the AI Era

title: Apple’s new M5 MacBook Air doubles storage and speeds up AI

The updated models come with double the base storage at 512GB, support Wi-Fi 7 and Bluetooth 6, and include features like a Liquid Retina display, long battery life, and Spatial Audio. title: New MacBook Pro chips bring 4x AI power for creative pros
description: Apple has introduced its new M5 Pro and M5 Max chips, specifically designed to power the latest MacBook Pro models. These chips utilize a novel Fusion Architecture, combining two dies into a single system-on-a-chip, significantly boosting CPU and GPU performance. With Neural Accelerators in each GPU core and higher unified memory bandwidth, the M5 Pro and M5 Max offer over four times the peak AI compute compared to the previous generation, making them ideal for professionals in demanding fields like 3D animation and AI research.

title: Craig Barratt to succeed Frank Yeary as Intel (INTC) board chair after meeting
description: Intel Corporation announced that its Board Chair, Frank D. Yeary, will retire and not seek re-election at the 2026 Annual Meeting of Stockholders. Dr. Craig H. title: Bank of Montreal (BERZ) issues 3‑year Buffer Enhanced Notes, capped 63.6% return
description: Bank of Montreal (BMO) has priced US$1,027,000 Senior Medium-Term Buffer Enhanced Return Notes due March 05, 2029, linked to an equally weighted basket of seven tech equities (AAPL, AMZN, GOOG, META, MSFT, NVDA, TSLA). These notes offer 1:1 upside exposure up to a maximum redemption amount of $1,636.00 per $1,000 principal, representing a 63.60% return, and include a 10.00% downside buffer. Investors face potential losses of up to 90.00% if the basket declines significantly beyond the buffer, and all payments are subject to BMO's credit risk.

While its valuation is no longer "cheap," Garmin's technological moats in aviation and marine, coupled with opportunities in automotive and subscriptions, position it as a quality play for diversified portfolios, even with competitive and macroeconomic challenges.

title: How iPhone 17e becomes a rugged warehouse barcode scanner
description: Socket Mobile announced that its industrial scanning solutions, including XtremeScan, DuraSled, and DuraScan Terminal, are now compatible with Apple's new iPhone 17e. This collaboration enables businesses to use the enhanced durability and performance of the iPhone 17e for 1D/2D barcode capture in demanding environments across various industries. The company's CaptureSDK facilitates easy integration for developers, allowing for quick deployment of iPhone 17e-based data capture systems.

author: MarketMinute or NULL
title: T-Mobile Unveils "Spring Surge" Promos: iPhone 17e and iPad Air Bundles Signal Shift Toward Ecosystem ARPU
description: T-Mobile has launched its "Spring Surge" promotions, bundling the new iPhone 17e and M4 iPad Air with its premium "Experience Beyond" service plans. This strategy aims to shift revenue growth from subscriber counts to increased "device-attach" rates and Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) by making Apple's ecosystem more accessible through higher-tier plans that no longer include taxes and fees. T-Mobile, Apple, Verizon, and AT&T are positioned differently in this new competitive landscape, where T-Mobile's move is seen as a calculated risk to secure long-term gains in the evolving 5G market.

author: Börse Global (en)
title: Analyzing the John Hancock Multifactor Large Cap ETF's Investment Approach ()
description: The John Hancock Multifactor Large Cap ETF (JHML) manages approximately $1.1 billion and uses a systematic, rules-based strategy to invest in the U.S. equity market, focusing on factors like quality, value, momentum, and firm size rather than just market capitalization. Its portfolio is diversified across sectors, with a significant allocation to technology, including major holdings like NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Amazon. The fund aims to outperform conventional market-cap-weighted indexes by balancing exposure across multiple return drivers, especially during volatile periods for large technology stocks, and has a competitive expense ratio of 0.29% with semi-annual rebalancing.

author: José Adorno
title: 6 Major Mac Features The MacBook Neo Doesn't Have
description: Apple has introduced the MacBook Neo, a budget-friendly Mac targeting students and those needing a computer for lightweight tasks, starting at $599. While it offers a powerful A18 Pro chip from iPhones, it makes significant compromises compared to other Macs, lacking an M-series chip, extensive customization options, a mini-LED display, faster ports, and MagSafe with fast charging. These trade-offs are necessary to achieve its lower price point, but they limit memory to 8GB, narrow port selection, and offer a less vibrant display.
